methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in AI & Software (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 38 same-sector peers exist in AI & Software, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in AI & Compute

data.narrative:
Kyutai is a growth-stage AI & Software company that has raised €300M to date, founded by Xavier Niel. The relevant comp universe spans the AI & Software sector, encompassing 1 public company (Palantir, ticker PLTR) and 8 private peers including OpenAI, xAI, Anthropic, and Databricks.

Standard peer-median valuation proves inapplicable here. The AI & Software sector contains 38 same-sector comparables, but rigorous filtering by stage, adjacency, and valuation tier yields fewer than three datapoints in any narrow cohort. Applying an all-sector median would introduce unacceptable noise given Kyutai's specific position within frontier AI infrastructure. Consequently, triangulation via traditional multiples analysis cannot be reliably executed with the available data.

The analysis exposes a fundamental constraint common in frontier-tech valuation: the absence of sufficient homogeneous precedent. With only one public peer and a private universe dominated by earlier-stage or later-stage entities, conventional approaches break down. Any valuation estimate would require either (1) subjective weighting of the single public anchor (PLTR), (2) private transaction data from the peer set that is not provided here, or (3) bottom-up cost-of-capital and revenue projection frameworks not present in this dataset. Without these inputs, a defensible range cannot be synthesized. Key uncertainties include the maturity profile relative to late-stage AI companies and whether Kyutai's capital efficiency and technology differentiation support a premium or discount to sector norms.
